title: Chapter 23: Magic Mirror, Break Their Faces
content: Fifty thousand faces.

That was what Nova had pulled from the FaceVault database. Fifty thousand women's faces, scraped and stolen and rearranged into someone else's fantasy, someone else's profit. Fifty thousand lives turned into product without consent, without compensation, without even the courtesy of knowing.

She could not release the images. She'd already made that calculation — had made it before she ever breached the honeypot, before Chloe's trap had snapped shut and she'd ripped herself free leaving digital blood on the walls. Releasing the images would re-victimize every single one of those women. It would be the same violation, different vector. Nova wasn't in the business of doubling trauma.
But she could destroy Piper Brandt.

She sat cross-legged on the cold floor of the storage unit, three monitors casting her face in pale blue light, fingers hovering over the keyboard. The safe house smelled like stale air and copper — the latter from the makeshift server rack Virgil had guided her to repurpose from an old HVAC unit. Classy. Very cyberpunk-chic.

"Piper Brandt," she said, pulling up his file. Young. White. Tech-bro in the most literal sense — former AI startup cofounder, current deepfake marketplace kingpin. FaceVault was his baby. His pride and joy. His revenue stream.

"Want me to run the numbers?" Virgil's voice crackled through the speakers, Y2K pixelated and warm. "Because I've been running the numbers, and this man made four point seven million dollars in the last fiscal year off other people's faces."
"Run the magic mirror," Nova said.

The magic mirror. She'd built the framework weeks ago, back when Circle 8 was still a shape on the horizon. The concept was elegant: take the target's own face and mirror it back at them — reshaped, remixed, recontextualized into every humiliating scenario they'd forced upon others. The punishment fits the crime. The mirror reflects.
But Nova had lines.

No sexual deepfakes. Not ever. Not even for Piper Brandt, who deserved every nightmare the universe could conjure. Because using sexual humiliation as a weapon would make her no different from him, and the whole goddamn point was that she WAS different. So instead: Piper-as-clown. Piper-as-crying-baby. Piper-as-losing-contestant on a game show that didn't exist, sobbing into a oversized microphone while a cartoon audience pointed and laughed. Piper in a dunce cap. Piper slipping on a banana peel in a loop that never ended. Piper, chubby-cheeked and wailing, diaper and all.
Absurd. Degrading. Impossible to ignore.
And on every single public-facing screen in a two-mile radius of Piper Brandt's apartment building and his downtown office.

"Executing magic mirror," Virgil confirmed. The AI's satisfaction was audible, a digital purr. "Billboards first. Then mall screens. Then lobby displays. I've got access to twenty-three digital out-of-home installations, fourteen internal commercial displays, and — oh, this is fun — the digital directory in his own apartment building."
"Do it."
The screens lit up at 8:47 PM, peak evening foot traffic in downtown LA.

On the Jumbotron-size billboard at Sunset and Vine, Piper Brandt's face appeared — smooth, confident, that irritating tech-bro grin — and then morphed. The smile stretched too wide. Red nose bloomed. Clown wig erupted from his scalp. The animation was seamless, thirty frames per second of humiliation rendered in full HD. Beneath him, text scrolled: THIS IS WHAT IT FEELS LIKE.

Across the street, the digital bus shelter cycled to Piper-as-toddler, red-faced and mid-tantrum, pounding tiny fists against a playroom floor. Pedestrians stopped. Pointed. Someone filmed. Of course someone filmed.

At the Westfield Century City mall, food court screens showed Piper in a dunce cap, sitting in the corner of a classroom while animated children threw paper at him. Shoppers gasped, laughed, stared in confused delight.

And at the Lumina Luxury Apartments — Piper's building — the lobby directory display forgot about floor numbers and instead offered a five-minute loop of Piper Brandt losing a spelling bee, getting pied in the face, and crying at a fictional awards ceremony where the announcer declared, "And the award for Worst Person goes to..."
His own neighbors saw it first.
Simultaneously — because Nova didn't do anything by halves — FaceVault went dark.

Every server. Every mirror. Every cached copy on every cloud backup across four continents. The scrub was surgical, thorough, and absolute. Virgil had spent three weeks writing the deletion algorithms, mapping every server location, every redundant backup, every CDN edge node where those images lived. Nothing survived.
And then the notifications went out.
Fifty thousand emails, landing in fifty thousand inboxes:
*Your images have been removed from FaceVault and all known mirrors. A data deletion certificate has been generated for your records.*
Not a warning. Not a threat. A certificate.

Nova had built this feature specifically for Circle 8. She'd thought about it for days — what victims actually needed. Sympathy? Sure. Justice? Eventually. But right now, in the immediate aftermath? They needed documents. Paper trails. Proof that the images were gone, timestamped and authenticated, suitable for victim impact statements and court filings and cease-and-desist letters and peace of mind. The certificate included the deletion timestamp, the server IDs from which images were removed, a cryptographic hash verifying the scrub, and a legally vetted statement confirming the data had been permanently destroyed.
"Deletion certificates sent," Virgil reported. "Fifty thousand. Zero bounces."

"Good," Nova said quietly. She stared at the confirmation numbers scrolling across her middle monitor — green checkmarks, row after row after row. Like watching a wound close. "That's the part that matters."
"The humiliation is also fun," Virgil offered.
"The humiliation is the announcement. The certificates are the actual work."
"And yet you're smiling."
Nova touched her face. She was. Just barely. "Shut up."

Piper Brandt stumbled out of the Lumina Luxury Apartments at 9:12 PM, red-faced and hyperventilating, one hand covering his face like that would help. The lobby had been bad enough — his own building's directory showing him in a diaper — but outside was worse. The billboard across the street. The bus shelter. The restaurant's outdoor menu screen, which had somehow been commandeered to show him crying over a spilled ice cream cone.
"TURN IT OFF!" he screamed at no one. At everyone. At the indifferent digital sky. "TURN IT THE F*** OFF!"

A small crowd had gathered. Not a mob — just people, walking dogs, coming home from dinner, existing in the world — and they were watching Piper Brandt's face do things he'd never consented to on every available screen, and they were laughing, and filming, and someone near the back was saying, "Isn't that the FaceVault guy?" and someone else was saying, "Karma's a bitch," and someone else was saying nothing at all, just watching with the quiet satisfaction of someone who understood exactly what this was.

Piper fumbled for his phone. Dialed. Nothing. Dialed again. His contacts had been scrubbed — not deleted, not exactly, but rearranged into uselessness. Every entry now showed the same number: a suicide prevention hotline. Even the ones that weren't numbers. His therapist's name redirected to the hotline. His lawyer. His mother.

He stood on the sidewalk in front of his building, phone pressed to his ear, listening to a calm voice say, "You've reached the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline. We're here to help," and across the street, on the biggest billboard in the neighborhood, his own face wept cartoon tears the size of basketballs.

The screens displayed for exactly one hour. Nova had checked — three different legal databases, two pro bono digital rights attorneys whose public writing she'd consulted without contacting them. One hour was long enough for evidence. Long enough for witnesses. Long enough for Piper Brandt to understand, viscerally, what it felt like to have your image used without your consent.
Short enough to be defensible.
At 9:47 PM, every screen snapped back to its original programming. Billboards showed car ads. Bus shelters showed movie posters. The Lumina building directory went back to listing unit numbers.
But the internet is forever, and Piper Brandt had been filmed by a hundred strangers, and the clips were already spreading.

Across the country, in a rent-controlled apartment in Washington DC where the radiator clanked like it was trying to communicate — Marcus Chen was looking at something that made his blood crystallize in his veins.

He'd been following the NightWatch thread for days. NightWatch Solutions, the surveillance contractor linked to Sterling Minds, the private intelligence firm that kept turning up in the margins of every circle Nova had burned. Marcus had accessed their database through a backdoor he'd found in the Paradise Cove billing records — the same breach Nova had used, but he'd gone deeper, laterally, into systems she hadn't touched because she'd been busy surviving Chloe's honeypot.
NightWatch Solutions wasn't just a surveillance company.
It was a personal intelligence operation.

Ten thousand dossiers. Activists. Journalists. Influencers. Politicians. Labor organizers. Environmental campaigners. Anyone who might be a threat to Sterling Minds' interests — which, as far as Marcus could tell, were the interests of everyone with enough money to hire a private intelligence firm. Each dossier was a miniature biography: financial records, social media archives, known associates, vulnerabilities. Not just data. Weaponized data. Blueprints for destroying people.
And buried in the NightWatch database, sandwiched between a labor organizer in Milwaukee and a state senator from Arizona, was a file labeled:
**VALERIUS, N.**
Marcus stared at it.
He clicked.

The dossier was thin — thinner than most, which meant it was either low-priority or highly classified. Basic biographical data. Age: 22. Location: Los Angeles. Occupation: listed as "Social media influencer / Digital content creator," which was technically true and completely incomplete. Known associates: redacted. Risk assessment: HIGH. Monitoring status: ACTIVE.
But the name. The actual name.
Not "The Phantom." Not a handle or an alias or a digital ghost story.
Nova Valerius.
Marcus sat back in his chair. The radiator clanked. The apartment was cold — he'd forgotten to pay the heating bill again — but the chill he felt wasn't temperature.
They knew her name.

Not the legend. Not the rumor. Her. The actual person, the twenty-two-year-old woman behind the most effective digital vigilante operation in recent history, and Sterling Minds had her in a database labeled with the cold bureaucratic efficiency of a company that destroyed people for a living.

He thought about his editor. Margaret. Killed in what the police called a "traffic incident" and what Marcus knew was a message. He thought about the way these people operated — not with guns, usually, but with information. Leverage. The slow, systematic demolition of a person's life until they either shut up or disappeared.

He thought about Nova, alone in whatever safe house she was hiding in, executing takedowns with the precision of a surgeon, unaware that the people she was fighting had already identified her operating room.
He opened the Phantom Network relay. Typed fast, not because he didn't care about precision but because precision didn't matter when the building was on fire.
In Ohio, a woman named Diane sat at her kitchen table and opened her email.

She'd been checking her inbox every hour for three weeks — a compulsion she hated but couldn't stop — because every time she looked, she expected to find new photos of herself. New violations. New evidence that Piper Brandt or someone like him had found new ways to use her face without permission.
Instead, she found a certificate.
*Your images have been removed from FaceVault and all known mirrors. A data deletion certificate has been generated for your records.*
She read it twice. Three times. Then she printed it.

The printer was old and slow and the paper came out warm. She held it in both hands. The certificate had her name on it. A timestamp. A cryptographic hash she didn't understand but didn't need to — the fact that it existed, that someone had generated formal, documented, legally cognizable proof that her images were gone, that she could hold this paper in her hands and say "this happened and it's over" —

She printed a second copy. She went to the hall closet and found the frame she'd been saving — a simple black frame, nothing fancy, the kind you buy at Target and feel vaguely ashamed about — and she put the certificate inside it, and she hung it on the wall next to her degree, and she stood in her hallway at 10 PM on a Sunday night and cried.
Not sad tears. Not really. The kind of tears that come when something you thought would never end finally, impossibly, does.
The message arrived at 2:14 AM.

Nova was still in the storage unit, still cross-legged on the floor, monitors still casting her in blue. The Circle 8 folder was closed — sealed, delivered, done. She'd been sitting in the quiet after, letting the silence fill the space where the work had been, when Virgil pinged.
"Incoming. Phantom Network relay. Priority: URGENT."
She opened it.
*They know your name. Not the Phantom — YOU. Nova Valerius. NightWatch has a file. You need to move. NOW.*
— M
She read it twice.

Nova Valerius. Her real name. In a database built by Sterling Minds, the same company that had built the Circles of Hell she'd been burning down one by one. Chloe's company. The company that had already tried to trap her, to identify her, to put her in a box.
They'd succeeded.
Not completely. Not yet. But they had a name, and a name was a trail, and a trail was a map.
She reached out and touched the wall of the storage unit — the cold, windowless, concrete wall. Her fingers pressed against it like she was checking if it was real. If any of this was real.
"They've always been closer than we thought," Virgil said, his voice stripped of its usual sardonic warmth. Just the facts. Just the cold, clean facts. "Now they have a map."

Nova didn't panic. Panic was a luxury for people with time, and she didn't have any. She breathed once. Twice. Then her fingers were back on the keyboard, pulling up exit strategies, safe house rotations, dead drops she'd pre-positioned months ago for exactly this scenario.

"If they have my name, I need to move faster." Her voice was steady. Controlled. The voice of someone who had been running for so long that running was just another mode of being. "Circle 8 is done. One circle left."
Virgil processed this. She could hear him computing — the soft electronic hum that passed for a sigh in his architecture.
"Circle 9," he said. "The innermost. The deepest."
"The one at the center of everything."
"The one where Chloe lives."

Nova looked at the monitors. At the green checkmarks from the deletion certificates. At the quiet, unglamorous, essential work of giving fifty thousand women their faces back. At the satisfaction of it — real, earned, bittersweet, because the war wasn't over and might never be over, but this battle was won, and the women were free, and Piper Brandt would never be the same, and somewhere in Ohio, someone was hanging a certificate on her wall and crying tears that tasted like the beginning of something instead of the endless middle.
And then she looked at Marcus's message, still glowing on her screen.
*You need to move. NOW.*
"Circle 9," Nova said. "Let's burn it to the ground."
But even as she said it — even as the steel in her voice held firm — she could feel the walls of the storage unit pressing in. Cold. Windowless. A box she'd chosen, but a box nonetheless.
And somewhere in a database she couldn't reach, her name sat in a file, waiting.
